items.name: Carlisle SynTec Roofing Systems in Laredo, TX
items.meta: How Carlisle SynTec's TPO, EPDM, and PVC product lines fit Laredo commercial roofing projects, and what their manufacturer warranty paperwork covers.

sections.html:
Carlisle SynTec Systems on Laredo Commercial Roofs

Carlisle SynTec is one of the manufacturers whose membrane systems show up regularly on our proposals, particularly on TPO and EPDM jobs. The company is based in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, and operates as part of Carlisle Companies, a large diversified building-products manufacturer. We work with their product lines the same way we work with several other manufacturers - matching the system to the building, not the building to a preferred brand.

Where the Company Started

Carlisle SynTec built its reputation initially on EPDM rubber roofing, a product category the broader Carlisle organization was an early and significant manufacturer of decades ago. That EPDM history is part of why the company's rubber membrane line remains a substantial part of its current commercial roofing catalog, alongside newer TPO and PVC product lines developed as the single-ply market shifted toward those materials.

The Product Lines We Spec

Carlisle's current commercial catalog spans EPDM, TPO, and PVC single-ply membranes in various thicknesses, along with insulation, fastening accessories, and coating products meant to work as a complete roof assembly rather than a membrane sold in isolation. On a Laredo job we'll pull from whichever part of that catalog fits the building - a white TPO membrane for a warehouse roof, an EPDM system for a building with deck-movement concerns, and so on.

Insulation and Accessory Products Alongside the Membrane

Beyond membrane itself, Carlisle manufactures a full line of insulation board, cover boards, fastening plates, and edge metal designed to be specified together as a single documented system rather than sourced piecemeal from different suppliers. Specifying components from one manufacturer's compatible catalog simplifies the warranty conversation considerably, since a mixed-source assembly can raise questions about which company is responsible if a failure occurs at the interface between two different manufacturers' products. We default to matched systems for exactly that reason unless there's a specific cost or availability issue that argues otherwise.

EPDM Roots and What Changed

The broader single-ply roofing market has moved substantially toward TPO over the last couple of decades, largely for reflectivity and heat-welded seam reasons that matter a lot in a climate like Laredo's. Carlisle's EPDM line remains available and supported, but the majority of new commercial roofing specifications we write today, across manufacturers generally, lean toward TPO or PVC rather than EPDM, for the reasons covered on our EPDM roof-systems page.

Reading a Carlisle Warranty Document

Manufacturer warranties on single-ply roofing typically cover the membrane material itself against manufacturing defects for a specified term, with separate terms sometimes available for labor or full-system coverage depending on how the project is contracted and installed. The specific terms, exclusions, and required maintenance obligations vary by product line and by the individual project's documentation, so we review the actual warranty language with the owner rather than summarizing it generically - warranty terms are a legal document, not a marketing summary, and we treat them that way.

  • Membrane type and thickness matched to the building's traffic and exposure profile
  • Insulation and fastening components sourced as a compatible system, not mixed ad hoc
  • Installation documented to the standard the manufacturer's warranty terms require
  • Warranty registration paperwork filed promptly after project completion
  • Product data sheets kept on file for the owner's future reference

Fitting Carlisle Into a Laredo Job

We don't default to any single manufacturer regardless of the building. Carlisle's product range is broad enough to cover most of what we spec on a typical Laredo commercial roof, and their distribution and product data availability make it straightforward to document a job to their specification. Where a different manufacturer's specific product - a particular coating chemistry, a specific PVC formulation - fits a building's needs better, we'll spec that instead.

On cross-dock warehouse projects in Killam and Unitec specifically, Carlisle's fully documented TPO system - membrane, insulation, and fastening all specified together - is a common baseline we compare other manufacturers' proposals against, simply because it's well established and thoroughly documented in the field.

On smaller retail and QSR jobs, cost and current local distributor stock usually matter more than any specific brand advantage, and we'll shop Carlisle against Versico, GAF, or another manufacturer on those projects rather than assuming Carlisle is automatically the least expensive option.

Questions About Carlisle SynTec Systems

Is Carlisle the manufacturer you recommend most often?

We spec from several manufacturers depending on the building and the specific product needed; Carlisle's broad single-ply catalog makes it a frequent fit, but not an automatic default.

Does Carlisle still make EPDM roofing?

Yes, EPDM remains part of their current product catalog alongside TPO and PVC, though most new commercial specifications we write today lean toward TPO or PVC for the climate-specific reasons discussed on our roof-systems pages.

What does a Carlisle product warranty actually cover?

That depends on the specific product line and project documentation; we review the actual warranty terms with the owner rather than generalizing, since exclusions and maintenance requirements vary by product.

Can Carlisle products be mixed with another manufacturer's insulation or fasteners?

We generally source components as a matched system per the manufacturer's specification, since mixing components across manufacturers can create warranty complications that aren't worth the marginal cost savings.

Where does Carlisle SynTec manufacture its products?

Carlisle SynTec is headquartered in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, and operates as part of the larger Carlisle Companies building-products organization, which also owns Versico as a separate roofing brand.

items.name: GAF Commercial Roofing Systems in Laredo, TX
items.meta: GAF's commercial TPO, modified bitumen, and coating product lines evaluated honestly for Laredo warehouse, retail, and QSR roofing projects.

sections.html:
GAF's Commercial Division on Laredo Roofs

GAF is best known nationally as the largest shingle manufacturer for residential roofing, but the company also runs a substantial commercial roofing division with its own single-ply, built-up, and coating product lines. We spec GAF commercial products on a portion of our Laredo jobs, particularly where their specific TPO or coating line fits the project better than an alternative manufacturer's.

A Residential Giant's Commercial Side

GAF is headquartered in Parsippany, New Jersey, and its name recognition comes overwhelmingly from residential asphalt shingles. The commercial division operates as a separate product and specification track aimed at low-slope roofing - TPO, modified bitumen, and built-up systems - rather than the pitched-roof shingle products the brand is best known for. It's worth understanding that distinction, since a manufacturer's residential reputation doesn't automatically say much about their commercial single-ply product quality, and we evaluate the two sides of the business independently when we're deciding whether to spec their products on a given job.

EverGuard and the Rest of the Commercial Line

GAF's commercial TPO product line, marketed under names like EverGuard, covers standard thickness options similar to what other major manufacturers offer, along with modified bitumen and built-up roofing products for buildings where those systems fit better than single-ply membrane. Their catalog also includes cover boards, insulation, and fastening accessories meant to be specified together as a complete assembly rather than sourced piecemeal from unrelated suppliers.

Built-Up and Modified Bitumen Products

Beyond single-ply TPO, GAF's commercial line includes built-up roofing and modified bitumen products that see use on some of the older downtown Laredo buildings where those systems are the better structural match, similar to the modified bitumen work described on our SBS roof-systems page. Having a manufacturer with both a modern single-ply catalog and an established built-up and modified bitumen line lets us keep a consistent manufacturer relationship on a mixed portfolio of properties rather than juggling separate suppliers for every roof type an owner manages.

Coatings and Recover Products

Beyond new membrane systems, GAF's commercial line includes reflective coating products used in recoat and recover projects, similar to the coating work described on our silicone roof coating page. We evaluate GAF's coating products against other manufacturers' options the same way we evaluate their membrane products - on fit for the specific roof, not brand preference.

  • Product line reviewed against the building's specific traffic and exposure profile
  • Membrane thickness selected to match anticipated roof use, not a default option
  • Coating or recover products evaluated against alternatives from other manufacturers
  • Component compatibility confirmed before mixing accessories across product lines
  • Warranty documentation filed and explained to the owner in plain terms

Where GAF Commercial Fits Alongside Other Manufacturers

We don't treat GAF as a default or a fallback; their commercial products get specified when they're the right fit for a particular roof, the same as Carlisle, Versico, Johns Manville, or any other manufacturer we work with. Product availability, specific technical requirements, and sometimes owner preference from a prior project all factor into which manufacturer we recommend for a given job.

Because GAF's national footprint depends primarily on its residential shingle distribution network, commercial product availability in a market the size of Laredo can vary more by season and by specific product than it does for a manufacturer whose entire business centers on commercial single-ply. We confirm current lead times on the specific GAF commercial product before committing to it on a project with a firm schedule, rather than assuming national scale automatically means fast local availability.

Every manufacturer covered on our manufacturers pages competes in roughly the same commercial single-ply and modified bitumen categories, and GAF's specific advantage on a given bid usually comes down to a particular product's price at the time, a distributor's current stock in this region, or occasionally an owner's existing relationship with the brand from a prior project elsewhere in their portfolio. We present GAF alongside Carlisle, Versico, Johns Manville, Sika Sarnafil, and Elevate in every proposal where more than one manufacturer's product would reasonably work, rather than steering an owner toward one name without showing the full comparison in writing.

Reading a GAF Commercial Warranty

Like other manufacturers, GAF's commercial warranty terms vary by product line and by how a project is documented and installed. We don't summarize warranty terms generically in a proposal - we pull the actual document for the specific product being installed and go through it with the owner, since exclusions and maintenance obligations differ enough between product lines that a generic summary would be misleading.

Questions About GAF Commercial Roofing

Is GAF's commercial roofing the same quality as their residential shingles?

They're different product lines built to different specifications for different roof types; we evaluate GAF's commercial products on their own merits rather than assuming residential reputation carries over.

What's EverGuard?

It's the brand name under which GAF markets part of its commercial TPO membrane line.

Do you recommend GAF over other manufacturers?

We recommend whichever manufacturer's product fits the specific building and overall budget best; GAF is one of several manufacturers we regularly work with, not a default choice.

Does GAF offer recover and coating products for existing roofs?

Yes, their commercial line includes reflective coating and recover products alongside their new membrane systems.

Where is GAF headquartered?

Parsippany, New Jersey, though the commercial roofing division's manufacturing and distribution operations are spread across multiple facilities nationally.

items.name: Holcim Elevate Roofing Systems in Laredo, TX
items.meta: What the Firestone-to-Elevate rebrand under Holcim means for Laredo building owners, and how their current EPDM and TPO lines are specified.

sections.html:
Elevate Roofing Systems, Formerly Firestone Building Products

Elevate is the current name for what was, until a 2022 rebrand, Firestone Building Products. Swiss building-materials company Holcim acquired the business and renamed it, but the product lines and manufacturing behind the name are largely a continuation of Firestone's long-running commercial roofing catalog. We still occasionally hear owners ask for Firestone by name, and we explain the rebrand plainly when that comes up.

The Rebrand and Why It Happened

Firestone Building Products had operated as a distinct commercial roofing manufacturer for decades, built substantially on the strength of its EPDM rubber roofing line - Firestone was one of the earliest and largest EPDM manufacturers in the country. When Holcim acquired the business, it rebranded the company as Elevate as part of folding it into Holcim's broader building-materials portfolio. The manufacturing facilities, much of the technical staff, and the core product formulations carried over through that transition.

What Actually Changed and What Didn't

The name on the roll of membrane changed; the underlying product chemistry for existing lines largely did not change overnight. Product names and packaging have been updated over time under the new Elevate brand, and new products have been introduced under Holcim's ownership, but a roof installed under the Firestone name and a comparable current Elevate product are typically closer cousins than an owner might assume from the branding change alone. We verify current product specifications directly rather than assuming anything carried over unchanged.

Holcim's Broader Building-Materials Business

Holcim itself is a large, diversified building-materials company with operations well beyond roofing membrane, spanning cement, aggregates, and other construction materials internationally. That scale doesn't change what shows up on a Laredo roof deck, but it does mean the Elevate roofing business now sits inside a larger corporate structure than Firestone Building Products operated as on its own, which is worth an owner knowing when they're researching who actually stands behind a warranty decades from now.

The EPDM and TPO Product Lines

Elevate's current catalog includes EPDM in both black and white formulations, TPO membrane, and a range of insulation and coating products, continuing the breadth Firestone offered previously. Their EPDM line in particular remains one of the more established options in that category, given the company's decades of manufacturing history in rubber roofing specifically.

  • Current product documentation pulled directly from Elevate, not assumed from legacy Firestone literature
  • EPDM and TPO options evaluated against the building's specific needs
  • Warranty terms confirmed under the current Elevate program, not the prior Firestone terms
  • Component compatibility verified for any recover project involving older Firestone-branded material
  • Product data sheets kept on file under the current brand name for future reference

Warranty Paperwork After the Name Change

For roofs originally installed under Firestone's name that are still within a warranty term, the warranty obligations generally transferred to Elevate as the successor company, but we confirm the specific status of any older warranty directly with the manufacturer rather than assuming continuity, particularly on a re-cover or repair project involving a roof installed years before the rebrand.

What This Means for a Recover Project on an Older Firestone Roof

Laredo has a real number of buildings out there with an aging Firestone EPDM or TPO roof nearing the end of its service life. On a recover or partial replacement of one of those roofs, we check whether the existing membrane and any remaining warranty term are still tied to Firestone's original documentation or have been migrated into Elevate's current records, since that affects both the paperwork trail and which specification sheet governs compatible repair materials.

On new work, Elevate's EPDM line competes closely with Carlisle's on jobs where a building's deck condition calls for rubber membrane over TPO, and their TPO line competes with the same set of manufacturers covered elsewhere on our manufacturers pages. We don't treat the Firestone legacy as a reason to prefer Elevate on a new installation; we evaluate current product data, warranty terms, and pricing the same way we would for any manufacturer without an established history on this specific building.

Contractors experienced with Firestone's older EPDM and TPO systems generally carry that field experience directly over to the current Elevate product line, since installation methods for the membrane itself didn't change with the rebrand - only the name on the roll and the paperwork behind it did.

Questions About Elevate and the Firestone Transition

Is Elevate the same company as Firestone Building Products?

It's the current name for that business following Holcim's 2022 acquisition and rebrand; the manufacturing and much of the product lineup carried over from Firestone.

Will my old Firestone warranty still be honored?

Warranty obligations generally transferred to Elevate as the successor company, but we confirm the specific status directly with the manufacturer for any active older warranty rather than simply assuming continuity.

Does Elevate still make EPDM roofing?

Yes, EPDM remains a core part of their current catalog in both black and white formulations, continuing Firestone's long EPDM manufacturing history.

Why did the name change to Elevate?

Holcim, the large Swiss building-materials company that acquired the Firestone Building Products business, rebranded it as part of integrating it into Holcim's broader portfolio.

Do you still have access to older Firestone product specifications if needed?

Yes, for recover or repair work on an older Firestone-branded roof, we pull the relevant legacy documentation and confirm current compatibility with Elevate's present product line.

items.name: Johns Manville Roofing Systems in Laredo, TX
items.meta: Johns Manville's TPO, PVC, and modified bitumen roofing lines, and how their long manufacturing history applies to Laredo commercial re-roofs.

sections.html:
Johns Manville Membrane Systems on Laredo Roofs

Johns Manville is one of the oldest manufacturers in the roofing industry, with a corporate history stretching back to the 1800s, and it operates today as a subsidiary of Berkshire Hathaway. That combination of long institutional history and a financially stable parent company is part of why their product lines show up regularly across our proposals, particularly on jobs where modified bitumen or built-up roofing is the right system alongside their single-ply offerings.

A Company That Predates Modern Roofing Chemistry

Johns Manville's roots go back to insulation and building materials manufacturing well before single-ply membranes existed as a product category, which means the company's built-up and modified bitumen roofing lines carry a manufacturing history that predates most of the current single-ply market. That history matters less for a warehouse roof going up today than for the older downtown Laredo buildings where a modified bitumen re-roof is the right call, where a manufacturer with decades of specific experience in that system carries real weight.

TPO, PVC, and the BUR/Modified Bitumen Side

Johns Manville's current catalog spans TPO and PVC single-ply membranes for standard warehouse and retail work, plus a substantial built-up and SBS modified bitumen line for buildings suited to those systems. Having a manufacturer that produces both categories under one roof lets us keep sourcing consistent on a project that mixes system types across additions or phased construction, which happens more often than owners expect on buildings that have been expanded piecemeal over the years.

Insulation Manufacturing Alongside Roofing

Johns Manville's broader business includes a substantial insulation manufacturing line separate from roofing membrane, which is a natural extension of the company's original building-materials focus. On a roof project that also involves an insulation upgrade - which comes up often on the older modified bitumen re-roofs described on our SBS page - sourcing compatible insulation and membrane from the same manufacturer simplifies the documentation and avoids questions about component compatibility that a mixed-manufacturer assembly can raise.

Berkshire Hathaway Ownership and Stability

Johns Manville has operated as a Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary for over two decades, which gives the company a financial stability that matters when an owner is relying on a multi-decade warranty term. A manufacturer's ability to still be in business and honoring warranty obligations fifteen or twenty years after installation is a real, practical consideration, beyond any brand-reputation talking point.

  • System type - single-ply, modified bitumen, or built-up - matched to the specific building and deck
  • Product specifications pulled current for the exact system being installed
  • Fire-watch and application method planned separately for any torch-applied component
  • Warranty terms reviewed in full with the owner before contract signing
  • Documentation filed to the manufacturer's specific registration requirements

Reading the Warranty Fine Print

As with every manufacturer we work with, Johns Manville's warranty terms vary by product line, installation method, and how the project is documented at completion. We don't summarize warranty coverage in general terms in a proposal; we pull the specific document for the product being installed and walk the owner through what's actually covered, what voids coverage, and what ongoing maintenance the warranty assumes.

Where We See Johns Manville Specified Most Often Locally

On our Laredo project list, Johns Manville tends to come up most on the older downtown re-roofs where modified bitumen is the right system, and on mixed-portfolio jobs where an owner wants one manufacturer relationship covering both a modified bitumen building and a newer TPO warehouse. It's less often our first call on a pure new-construction cross-dock warehouse, where several other manufacturers compete just as closely on price and lead time.

When we put together a proposal that includes multiple manufacturer options, Johns Manville's built-up and modified bitumen catalog is usually the deciding factor over a manufacturer like Carlisle or Versico that leans more heavily toward single-ply, not the TPO or PVC side of their business where the competition is closer to even. We tell owners plainly which part of a manufacturer's catalog is genuinely differentiated versus which part is comparable to what several other manufacturers also offer.

Insulation manufacturing is also a substantial part of Johns Manville's broader building-materials business, separate from their roofing membrane and modified bitumen lines, which is worth knowing on a project where insulation and membrane are both being specified from the same source.

Questions About Johns Manville Roofing Systems

How old is Johns Manville as a company?

The company's history in building materials manufacturing dates back to the 1800s, making it one of the older manufacturers currently active in commercial roofing.

Is Johns Manville only a modified bitumen manufacturer?

No, their current catalog includes TPO and PVC single-ply membranes alongside built-up and SBS modified bitumen systems.

Does Berkshire Hathaway ownership affect product quality?

We can't speak to internal manufacturing decisions, but the financial stability of a Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary is a relevant factor for owners relying on a long-term warranty from the company.

Would you use Johns Manville for a torch-applied modified bitumen roof downtown?

It's one of the manufacturers we'd consider for that application, evaluated against the specific building alongside other manufacturers with comparable product lines.

What should I ask about before choosing a Johns Manville warranty?

Ask specifically what voids the coverage, what maintenance and inspection obligations it assumes, and whether the warranty covers material only or material and labor - we go through all of that before a contract is signed.

items.name: Sika Sarnafil PVC Roofing in Laredo, TX
items.meta: Sika Sarnafil's PVC membrane line and long manufacturing track record, evaluated honestly for Laredo buildings that need chemical-resistant roofing.

sections.html:
Sarnafil PVC Systems Under the Sika Name

Sarnafil was a Swiss pioneer in PVC single-ply roofing membrane, and the product line now operates under Sika, the larger Swiss chemical and building-materials company that owns it. On Laredo jobs where PVC's chemical resistance is the deciding factor - buildings near diesel exposure, grease exhaust, or industrial process venting - Sarnafil is one of the manufacturers whose product we specify.

A Swiss PVC Pioneer

Sarnafil's roofing membrane business developed one of the longer manufacturing track records in PVC single-ply roofing specifically, predating a lot of the current single-ply market that later shifted heavily toward TPO. Sika, a large diversified chemical and construction-materials manufacturer, owns and continues the Sarnafil membrane line as part of its broader building-materials portfolio worldwide.

Sika's Broader Construction Chemicals Business

Sika's business extends well beyond roofing membrane into concrete admixtures, waterproofing, sealants, and other construction chemical products used across a wide range of building types. The Sarnafil roofing line is one division within that larger structure rather than a standalone company, which means Sarnafil benefits from Sika's overall corporate scale and research resources even though the roofing product itself is what shows up on the building.

The Membrane Line Itself

Sika Sarnafil's current PVC catalog includes membranes in the standard thickness ranges used across the industry, along with accessory products, fastening systems, and detailing components meant to be specified as a complete assembly. Their reinforced PVC sheets are hot-air welded the same way other manufacturers' PVC products are, and the field welding discipline described on our PVC roof-systems page applies equally to Sarnafil membrane.

Why Track Record Matters on a Multi-Decade Decision

A roof membrane is a decision an owner lives with for decades, and PVC specifically has a somewhat complicated reputation because older-generation PVC products from various manufacturers had plasticizer migration issues that made membranes brittle over time. A manufacturer with Sarnafil's specific length of manufacturing history in PVC gives an owner more field data to look at when asking how a particular formulation ages, compared to a newer entrant into the PVC category. We point owners to that manufacturing history as a factual data point, not a guarantee of performance.

  • PVC formulation and thickness matched to the building's specific chemical exposure risk
  • Field weld schedule planned around morning deck temperatures
  • Every seam probed and results logged by roof zone before project close-out
  • Flashing detail reviewed specifically at exhaust and process-vent penetrations
  • Warranty documentation reviewed with the owner before contract signing

Warranty Structure on Sarnafil Systems

Like every manufacturer we cover, Sika Sarnafil's warranty terms are specific to the product line and installation documentation for a given project. We pull the actual warranty document for the specific membrane being installed rather than describing coverage in general terms, since exclusions and maintenance obligations differ enough between product generations that a generic summary would misrepresent what's actually covered.

Where Sarnafil Fits Among Our Laredo PVC Jobs

Sarnafil competes directly with other PVC manufacturers on the buildings where PVC's chemical resistance is the reason we're specifying single-ply membrane at all - the truck-yard-adjacent warehouses, the restaurant exhaust roofs, the industrial process buildings described on our PVC roof-systems page. We compare Sarnafil's current product data and pricing against those alternatives on a job-by-job basis rather than treating it as an automatic first choice within the PVC category.

Beyond the membrane sheet itself, Sika Sarnafil's catalog includes prefabricated corner and detail pieces meant to speed up the labor-intensive part of PVC installation around curbs, pipe penetrations, and parapet corners. Using manufacturer-fabricated details instead of hand-forming every corner on site reduces the number of field welds a crew has to make in the first place, which matters on a hot Laredo roof where every additional field weld is another opportunity for a heat-related quality issue if the crew is rushing to beat the afternoon temperature.

Sika's broader business extends well beyond roofing membrane into concrete admixtures, waterproofing, and other construction chemical products, and we source accessories and detailing components from the same manufacturer as the membrane wherever possible, since mixing PVC formulations across manufacturers at a field weld can create compatibility questions that are better avoided than argued about later.

Questions About Sika Sarnafil PVC Roofing

Is Sarnafil a different product from other PVC membranes?

It's a distinct manufacturer's formulation with its own long manufacturing history in PVC roofing specifically, though it competes in the same general PVC single-ply category as other manufacturers' products.

Why choose Sarnafil over another PVC manufacturer?

We evaluate Sarnafil against other PVC manufacturers, including Johns Manville's and Sika's own broader catalog, on the same basis as any product decision - fit for the building's specific exposure and cost - rather than defaulting to one brand.

Has Sarnafil PVC had the plasticizer brittleness problems older PVC had?

That's a fair question to raise with the manufacturer directly for the specific product generation being installed on your building; we discuss it honestly rather than making a blanket claim either way.

Is Sika the same company as Sarnafil?

Sika is the parent company that owns and continues the Sarnafil roofing membrane line as part of its broader building-materials business, which also spans concrete admixtures and waterproofing products used well outside of roofing.

What does a Sarnafil warranty typically require for maintenance?

That varies by product line and project documentation; we review the specific warranty document with the owner rather than summarizing generically.

items.name: Versico Roofing Systems in Laredo, TX
items.meta: Versico's VersiFlex and VersiWeld single-ply product lines and independent-distributor model, evaluated for Laredo commercial roofing projects.

sections.html:
Versico Single-Ply Systems on Laredo Roofs

Versico is, like Carlisle SynTec, part of the Carlisle Companies building-products organization, operating as a related but separate brand with its own product lines and distribution approach. We spec Versico membrane on a portion of our jobs where their specific product or distributor availability fits the project better than an alternative manufacturer.

A Sister Company to Carlisle SynTec

Versico and Carlisle SynTec share a parent organization, which means there's real overlap in underlying manufacturing between the two brands, but they operate as distinct product lines sold through different channels. Understanding that relationship helps explain why some of Versico's product characteristics resemble Carlisle's while the brands are marketed and distributed separately, each with its own dealer network and its own warranty program administered independently of the other.

The VersiFlex and VersiWeld Product Lines

Versico markets its EPDM products under the VersiFlex name and its TPO and PVC single-ply lines under VersiWeld, alongside insulation, fastening, and coating products meant to round out a complete roofing assembly. The catalog breadth is comparable to what other major single-ply manufacturers offer - standard thickness options in TPO, EPDM, and PVC, sold as part of a documented system rather than membrane alone.

Versico's catalog beyond membrane includes polyisocyanurate insulation board, cover boards, fastening plates, and edge and termination metal designed to be specified as one matched system. As with any manufacturer on our list, we source these components together rather than mixing insulation from one supplier with membrane from another, since a mixed assembly can complicate a warranty claim later if a failure occurs at the boundary between two manufacturers' products.

Coatings and Recover Products

Versico also produces reflective coating products used in recover and recoat projects on existing roofs, similar in purpose to the coating work described on our silicone roof coating page. We evaluate their coating line against other manufacturers' options on the same basis as membrane - fit for the specific roof's condition and exposure, not brand loyalty.

Distributor Network Instead of Direct Sales

Versico has historically leaned on an independent distributor network for product sales rather than selling exclusively direct, which affects availability and lead times on certain products depending on regional distributor stock. We check current product availability before committing to a Versico spec on a job with a tight schedule, the same way we would with any manufacturer whose distribution model isn't direct-to-contractor.

  • Product line - VersiFlex vs. VersiWeld - matched to membrane type needed for the building
  • Current distributor availability checked before the spec is finalized for a tight-schedule job
  • Insulation and fastening components sourced as a matched system per specification
  • Field weld or seam work documented to the standard the warranty requires
  • Warranty registration completed promptly after project close-out

Warranty Structure on Versico Systems

Versico's warranty terms, like every manufacturer covered here, are specific to the product line, installation method, and project documentation. We review the actual warranty document for the specific system being installed with the owner directly, rather than describing coverage in general terms that might not match the specific product or project.

Where Versico Comes Up on Laredo Jobs

We tend to reach for Versico on jobs where distributor stock or lead time on a specific membrane thickness works out better than a competing manufacturer's at the time of the project, or where an owner already has other Versico-roofed buildings in a portfolio and wants to keep specifications consistent across properties. It's rarely the deciding factor on its own; it's one input among several, alongside cost, availability, and the building's specific exposure profile.

Because the two brands share manufacturing roots, an owner comparing a Versico VersiWeld quote against a Carlisle TPO quote is often comparing two products with more in common than the different names and marketing suggest. Where the real differences show up are current pricing, which distributor's stock is closer and faster for this specific job, and occasionally a small difference in accessory product design between the two catalogs. We walk owners through those specific differences rather than treating the brand names as interchangeable or as fundamentally different products.

Versico's own catalog beyond membrane includes polyisocyanurate insulation, cover boards, and fastening and edge-metal accessories meant to be specified alongside the membrane as one documented system, similar in scope to what Carlisle offers under its own name.

Questions About Versico Roofing Systems

Is Versico the same as Carlisle SynTec?

They share a parent company, Carlisle Companies, but operate as separate brands with distinct product lines and distribution channels.

What's the difference between VersiFlex and VersiWeld?

VersiFlex is Versico's EPDM product line, available in both black and white formulations; VersiWeld covers their TPO and PVC single-ply membranes across the standard thickness range.

Does Versico sell direct to contractors?

Versico has historically relied on independent distributors rather than exclusively direct sales, which we account for when checking product availability on a tight-schedule job before it becomes a problem mid-project.

Why would you choose Versico over Carlisle SynTec for a job?

The decision usually comes down to specific product availability, distributor lead time, or a particular product characteristic that fits the building better at the time of the project, rather than any fundamental performance gap between the two sister brands.

What does a Versico warranty cover?

That depends on the specific product line and how the project is installed and documented; we review the actual terms with the owner before any contract is signed.
